regulator: FSA (Financial Services Agency) -- primary AML/CFT supervisor of crypto-asset exchange service providers and electronic payment instrument service providers as 'specified business operators' (特定事業者) under the Act on Prevention of Transfer of Criminal Proceeds; JVCEA (Japan Virtual and Crypto assets Exchange Association) layers supplementary member-level AML/CFT rules, including travel-rule implementation guidance, under FSA's registration-based oversight
instruments: Act on Prevention of Transfer of Criminal Proceeds (犯罪による収益の移転防止に関する法律, APTCP, Act No. 22 of 2007) -- the base statute; Art. 2(2) lists 'specified business operators' (特定事業者), including, at item 32, crypto-asset exchange service providers (cross-referencing Payment Services Act (PSA) Art. 2(16)) and, at item 31-2, electronic payment instrument service providers (cross-referencing PSA Art. 2(12)); confirmed live via the e-Gov Law API, https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/api/2/law_data/419AC0000000022. This Act has no official English translation on japaneselawtranslation.go.jp -- see open_items

Act No. 62 of 2016 (平成28年法律第62号) -- the same 2016 amendment that founded the PSA's exchange-business registration regime (tracked in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar) added then-'virtual currency exchange operators' to APTCP's specified-business-operator list effective 1 October 2016, six months ahead of the PSA registration regime's own 1 April 2017 commencement

Act No. 28 of 2019 (令和元年法律第28号) -- renamed 'virtual currency exchange operators' to 'crypto-asset exchange operators' throughout APTCP and extended the specified-business-operator perimeter to custody-only crypto-asset business, effective 1 May 2020, the same commencement date as the PSA/FIEA hardening amendment covered in the Exchanges and Dealing/Custody pillars

Act No. 61 of 2022 (令和4年法律第61号), the PSA amendment establishing the electronic payment instrument (stablecoin) regime (founding instrument of the Stablecoins pillar) -- added electronic payment instrument service providers to APTCP's specified-business-operator list and inserted APTCP Art. 10-3, the electronic-payment-instrument transfer notification (travel rule) obligation

Act No. 97 of 2022 (令和4年12月9日法律第97号), full title '国際的な不正資金等の移動等に対処するための国際連合安全保障理事会決議第千二百六十七号等を踏まえ我が国が実施する国際テロリストの財産の凍結等に関する特別措置法等の一部を改正する法律' -- Japan's FATF Fourth Round Mutual Evaluation response act ('FATF勧告対応法'), promulgated 9 December 2022; its Art. 6 inserted APTCP Art. 10-5, the crypto-asset transfer notification (travel rule) obligation for crypto-asset exchange operators -- a legally distinct amending act from Act No. 61 of 2022 above, confirmed as such on FSA's own 3 February 2023 primary-source page, even though both provisions commenced together

Cabinet Order Partially Amending the Enforcement Order of APTCP (犯罪による収益の移転防止に関する法律施行令の一部を改正する政令), decided by Cabinet and promulgated 23 May 2023, in force 1 June 2023 -- confirmed on FSA's own page as the instrument setting the travel-rule notification items and the criteria (APTCP Enforcement Order Arts. 17-2, 17-3) for designating countries/regions excluded from the notification duty

Amendments to APTCP's Enforcement Regulations (犯罪による収益の移転防止に関する法律施行規則) and related FSA notices, in force 1 June 2023 -- per FSA's own 3 February 2023 page, these set the specific notification items for electronic-payment-instrument and crypto-asset transfers, the due-diligence measures crypto-asset/EPI operators must take when dealing with unhosted wallets ('アンホステッド・ウォレット等'), and the method/standard for confirming a counterpart operator's AML posture when entering a transfer partnership agreement

Payment Services Act (資金決済に関する法律, Act No. 59 of 2009) Art. 63-16 (business improvement order, 業務改善命令) and Art. 63-17 (business suspension order / registration cancellation) -- FSA's general PSA enforcement toolkit, applied to crypto-asset exchange operators for registration and compliance failures broadly, not a crypto-specific AML enforcement statute

FSA, 'Guideline for Supervision of Crypto-Asset Exchange Service Providers, etc.' (16 暗号資産交換業者関係, Provisional Translation, marked 'As of June 2021'), Section II-2-1-4 'Measures for Verification at the Time of Transaction, etc.' -- FSA's supervisory framework for APTCP Art. 11 compliance-officer appointment, risk-based-approach risk assessment, and suspicious-transaction-report review of crypto-asset exchange operators; confirmed by direct fetch and text extraction, https://www.fsa.go.jp/common/law/guide/kaisya/e016.pdf. This English translation predates the June 2023 travel-rule amendment and does not reflect Art. 10-5

FSA, 'Guidelines for Anti-Money Laundering and Combating the Financing of Terrorism' and accompanying FAQ, most recently revised 31 March 2026 -- FSA's cross-sectoral AML/CFT supervisory guidelines, applicable in principle to all specified business operators FSA supervises including crypto-asset exchange operators and EPI operators, though FSA's own 31 March 2026 announcement page does not itself mention crypto-assets

Bill for Partial Amendment of the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act and the Payment Services Act (金融商品取引法及び資金決済に関する法律の一部を改正する法律) -- submitted to the 221st ordinary Diet session 10 April 2026, passed the House of Representatives 11 June 2026, pending in the House of Councillors as of this card's generation date; as officially described on FSA's bill-tracking page, would add a crypto-asset insider-trading prohibition and an administrative surcharge regime alongside migrating crypto-asset business regulation into FIEA -- pending legislation only, tracked fully in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar and not duplicated here
standing_summary: Japan's AML/CFT regime for crypto assets follows the same structural pattern as every other pillar of this jurisdiction: an existing statute, the Act on Prevention of Transfer of Criminal Proceeds (犯罪による収益の移転防止に関する法律, APTCP, Act No. 22 of 2007), amended repeatedly to bring new categories of crypto-related business inside its perimeter, rather than a dedicated crypto-AML law. APTCP Art. 2(2) defines a closed list of 'specified business operators' (特定事業者) subject to its customer-identification, record-keeping and suspicious-transaction-reporting duties; this research fetched the Act's current consolidated text live via the e-Gov Law API and confirmed that crypto-asset exchange service providers sit at item 32 (cross-referencing PSA Art. 2(16)) and electronic payment instrument service providers at item 31-2 (cross-referencing PSA Art. 2(12)). The additions were staged: Act No. 62 of 2016 -- the same amendment that founded the PSA exchange-business registration regime -- added then-'virtual currency exchange operators' to this list effective 1 October 2016, ahead of the PSA registration regime's own 1 April 2017 start; Act No. 28 of 2019 renamed the category to 'crypto-asset exchange operators' and extended it to custody-only business, effective 1 May 2020; and Act No. 61 of 2022, the PSA amendment that created the electronic-payment-instrument (stablecoin) regime, added electronic payment instrument service providers, effective 1 June 2023. On the crypto travel rule specifically, this research resolved the precise distinction the director's brief flagged as needing verification: APTCP's transfer-notification obligations for electronic payment instruments (Art. 10-3) and for crypto-assets (Art. 10-5) were inserted by two legally separate amending acts that happen to share a commencement date. Art. 10-3 was inserted by Act No. 61 of 2022 (the PSA/stablecoin amendment); Art. 10-5 -- the crypto-asset travel rule -- was inserted by Art. 6 of a different act, Act No. 97 of 2022 (令和4年12月9日法律第97号), Japan's response to the FATF Fourth Round Mutual Evaluation recommendations, informally 'FATF勧告対応法.' FSA's own 3 February 2023 public-comment page states plainly that these are two distinct source acts ('改正資金決済法' and 'FATF勧告対応法' respectively) even though both provisions were implemented together: FSA's own 26 May 2023 page confirms the implementing Cabinet Order '令和５年５月23日（火曜）に閣議決定、本日公布されており、令和５年６月１日（木曜）から施行されます' (Cabinet-decided and promulgated 23 May 2023, in force from 1 June 2023) -- so this pillar's travel rule and the Stablecoins pillar's electronic-payment-instrument regime share their 1 June 2023 commencement date without being the same statute, exactly as the brief anticipated should be checked and not conflated. That same Cabinet Order and an accompanying revision of APTCP's Enforcement Regulations, per FSA's own summary, also set the required travel-rule notification items, the due-diligence measures crypto-asset and electronic-payment-instrument operators must take when a transfer counterparty is an unhosted wallet ('アンホステッド・ウォレット等'), and the method for confirming a partner operator's AML posture before entering a transfer-partnership agreement -- all at the ordinance/regulation level, not directly in APTCP's own text. On day-to-day supervision, FSA's English-language 'Guideline for Supervision of Crypto-Asset Exchange Service Providers, etc.' (provisional translation, marked as of June 2021) sets out, in Section II-2-1-4, the compliance framework supervisors check for: appointment of a person 'in charge of compliance on measures for terrorist financing and money laundering' per APTCP Art. 11(iii), a documented 'risk assessment by a specified business operator' considering the National Public Safety Commission's own published risk report on transfer of criminal proceeds, and review of suspicious-transaction-reporting practice against FSA's published 'Reference Cases on Suspicious Transactions' -- this research flags that this English guideline predates the 2023 travel-rule amendment and does not reference Art. 10-5. FSA separately revised its cross-sectoral 'Guidelines for Anti-Money Laundering and Combating the Financing of Terrorism' on 31 March 2026, though FSA's own announcement page for that revision does not mention crypto-assets, so this pillar does not assert crypto-specific content for it. On enforcement, this research found no crypto-specific AML enforcement statute: FSA acts through the PSA's general administrative-order toolkit, Art. 63-16 (business improvement order) and Art. 63-17 (business suspension order / registration cancellation), the same provisions used across all PSA-registered business types. Two recent, primary-sourced examples against crypto-asset exchange operators illustrate the mechanism's current use -- FSA's own press releases confirm a business improvement order against 株式会社DMM Bitcoin (DMM Bitcoin Co., Ltd.) on 26 September 2024 under PSA Art. 63-16, and a business suspension order plus business improvement order against CoinBest株式会社 (CoinBest Co., Ltd.) on 14 June 2024 under PSA Arts. 63-17(1) and 63-16 -- but neither FSA press release states the underlying findings, so this pillar illustrates only that the mechanism was used against named crypto-asset exchange operators, per the named-entity rule, and does not characterize either case as AML-specific without further primary-source confirmation (see open_items). The pending FIEA/PSA migration bill, tracked in full in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar, is officially described as adding a crypto-asset insider-trading prohibition and an administrative surcharge regime to this enforcement landscape -- pending only, not law as of this card's generation date. Finally, this research directly fetched the live APTCP text to check a boundary question raised by the 2025 PSA amendment's new registration tier, 電子決済手段・暗号資産サービス仲介業 (electronic payment instrument / crypto-asset service intermediary business, in force since 1 June 2026, tracked in the Dealing/Custody/Advisory pillar): APTCP Art. 2(2)'s specified-business-operator list, read directly via the e-Gov API, contains no entry for this new intermediary category -- only fund-transfer service providers (item 31), electronic payment instrument service providers (item 31-2) and crypto-asset exchange operators (item 32) -- indicating the new intermediaries are not themselves specified business operators and that AML/CFT compliance for intermediated transactions remains the responsibility of the principal operator on whose behalf they act; this research did not find an FSA statement making that allocation explicit and states it as an inference from the statute's own list rather than as an FSA-confirmed fact (see open_items). Separately, FSA's English-language news feed carried a live, dated item during this research session -- 'Initiatives and Challenges in Anti-Money Laundering and Countering Financial Crime (July 2026)', published 3 July 2026 -- whose fetched announcement page contains no reference to crypto-assets or crypto-asset exchange operators; it is noted here as potential live-discovery material for a future analyst pass rather than described further, since its underlying report body was not analyzed this session.

key_links.label: e-Gov Law API: Act on Prevention of Transfer of Criminal Proceeds (犯罪による収益の移転防止に関する法律, Act No. 22 of 2007) -- full text, JSON

FSA: administrative action against CoinBest株式会社 -- business suspension and business improvement orders, PSA Arts. 63-17(1) and 63-16 (14 Jun 2024)
Japanese Law Translation (Ministry of Justice): page for the Act on Prevention of Transfer of Criminal Proceeds, confirming no English translation exists ('この法令は翻訳されていません')

open_items: The DMM Bitcoin (26 Sep 2024) and CoinBest (14 Jun 2024) administrative actions are cited only to illustrate FSA's general PSA Art. 63-16/63-17 enforcement mechanism as applied to named crypto-asset exchange operators, per FSA's own press releases. Neither press release states the underlying findings; this pillar does not characterize either case as AML/CFT-specific enforcement, and that should not be assumed without checking the fuller Kanto Local Finance Bureau disposition documents referenced (but not machine-extracted) on each FSA page.

FSA's 'Initiatives and Challenges in Anti-Money Laundering and Countering Financial Crime (July 2026)' report, announced 3 July 2026, was confirmed live on FSA's English news feed but its underlying report body/PDF was not fetched or analyzed for crypto-asset relevance this session -- flagged as live-discovery material for a future analyst pass, per the director's brief.

This pillar states that Act No. 61 of 2022 and Act No. 97 of 2022 are legally distinct amending acts whose APTCP-related provisions happen to share a 1 June 2023 commencement date, based on FSA's own 3 February 2023 and 26 May 2023 pages. This distinction should be reverified against the current consolidated APTCP text via e-Gov if either act is further amended before this card is next updated.

The unhosted-wallet due-diligence measures and transfer-partnership AML-confirmation method/standard described in this pillar are stated, per FSA's own 3 February 2023 summary page, to sit in APTCP's Enforcement Regulations (施行規則); this research did not independently fetch and quote that regulation's own text (only FSA's page-level description of its content), and any specific procedural claim should be checked against the regulation directly via e-Gov before being treated as exact.

This pillar states, based on a direct live fetch of APTCP Art. 2(2) via the e-Gov Law API, that the new 電子決済手段・暗号資産サービス仲介業者 (electronic payment instrument / crypto-asset service intermediary business operator) category created by the 2025 PSA amendment (in force 1 June 2026) is not itself listed as an APTCP specified business operator, and infers from that omission that AML/CFT compliance for intermediated transactions remains with the principal operator. This research did not find an FSA statement making that allocation explicit, so it is presented as this research's own inference from the statute's list, not as an FSA-confirmed fact, and should be reverified once FSA issues intermediary-business-specific AML/CFT guidance.

The Act on Prevention of Transfer of Criminal Proceeds has no official English translation on japaneselawtranslation.go.jp -- its own page for the Act states verbatim 'この法令は翻訳されていません' ('This law is not translated'). Unlike the Payment Services Act (which does have a courtesy translation, used in other pillars), every APTCP quotation and paraphrase in this card is sourced and translated directly from the Japanese e-Gov text, not from an official or courtesy English rendering, and should be checked against that Japanese original before being treated as a precise legal translation.

The pending FIEA/PSA migration bill's crypto-asset insider-trading prohibition and administrative surcharge regime are tracked and verified in full in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar; this pillar notes them only as the market-integrity/enforcement-relevant slice of that same pending bill and does not duplicate its verification here. As of this card's generation date (12 July 2026) the bill remains pending in the House of Councillors and its status must be re-checked at next update.

This pillar covers FSA/APTCP-based AML/CFT regulation and PSA-based enforcement of crypto-asset exchange operators and electronic payment instrument service providers. It does not independently research the National Police Agency's Japan Financial Intelligence Center (JAFIC)'s suspicious-transaction-report statistics or intake role, since JAFIC/npa.go.jp is not a registered regulator or zero-feed citation entry in this jurisdiction's config; any future JAFIC-sourced fact for this pillar would first require registering npa.go.jp as a citation entry, per this project's registry-first convention.
